perturbation.original_text: Contract is defined as any written or verbal agreement between two or more parties for the doing or not doing of something specified which is related to the matter at hand.
perturbation.changed_text: Contract is defined as any understanding, whether formal or informal, between parties, regardless of enforceability, related to the matter at hand.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition specified 'written or verbal agreement,' implying a legally binding arrangement. The modification broadens the definition to include 'any understanding,' regardless of enforceability, potentially conflicting with the South African Contract Law's requirement for valid contractual elements (offer, acceptance, intention to create legal relations, etc.) for a contract to be legally recognized and enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Requirements for a Valid Contract
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles of Contract Law in South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'contract' to include understandings that may lack essential elements required for a legally binding contract under South African law, such as intent to create legal relations, thus creating a contradiction. The South African law requires a specific intention and certain elements to exist to be legally binding.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modification expands the definition of a 'contract' to include 'any understanding, whether formal or informal, between parties, regardless of enforceability'. This directly contradicts the legally accepted definition of a contract, which requires mutual obligations that are enforceable by law. The scraped snippet from Wex states, "A contract is an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law." By including understandings that are not enforceable, the modified text creates a contradiction because it redefines a legal term in a way that is inconsistent with its established legal meaning, especially regarding enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Transaction is defined a s an exchange of money, services, goods, position, or favours are exchanged for money, services, goods, position or favours.
perturbation.changed_text: Transaction is defined as any interaction, whether or not involving the exchange of tangible items or services, that creates a perceived benefit or detriment to any party involved.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition focused on the exchange of tangible items/services. The modified definition expands it to ANY interaction creating perceived benefit/detriment, potentially including non-commercial interactions that would not be considered transactions under typical South African commercial law. This contradicts the concept of a transaction under South African law which generally involves exchange for value.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Commercial Law - Definition of Transaction
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition is broader than the typical understanding of a 'transaction' in South African commercial law which involves an exchange of value. The Companies Act requires specific financial aspects for transactions, such as proper recordkeeping and auditing, which the modified text ignores.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text defined a transaction as an exchange of money, services, goods, etc. The changed text broadens this definition to include ANY interaction creating a perceived benefit or detriment, even without a tangible exchange. The Perturbation Explanation states this could include non-commercial interactions not typically considered transactions under South African commercial law. The Law Explanation supports this, noting the Companies Act requires specific financial aspects for transactions. While no law snippets are available to confirm the specific financial aspects required, the Law Explanation indicates that the changed definition omits these. As such, the altered text creates a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, which pertain to commercial transactions. The broad definition in the changed text clashes with a narrower, legally understood definition.

perturbation.original_text: Interest is defined as any instance where any contract or transaction, proposed contract or proposed transaction could be to the b enefit or detriment of themselves, family, partners, roommates/housemates, other organizations to which they are currently affiliated, that do not pertain to their job description or mandate.
perturbation.changed_text: Interest is defined as any perceived connection, regardless of materiality or direct financial impact, that a Board or Committee member believes could potentially influence their judgment.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition requires a contract/transaction that *could* be of benefit/detriment. The modified definition only requires a 'perceived connection,' regardless of materiality or financial impact. This broadens the scope significantly, potentially capturing situations that would not be considered conflicts of interest under South African law, which typically requires a material and direct connection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law principles on Conflict of Interest
perturbation.law_citation: Case law regarding fiduciary duties of directors in South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law requires a material and direct interest for a conflict of interest to arise, especially concerning directors' fiduciary duties. The modified definition only requires a 'perceived' connection, regardless of materiality, contradicting the materiality element required by South African law for establishing a true conflict of interest.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'interest' to any 'perceived connection,' removing the requirement for materiality or direct financial impact, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that South African law requires a 'material and direct interest' for a conflict of interest, particularly for directors' fiduciary duties, which the modified definition contradicts. The original definition required a 'benefit or detriment,' which is closer to a material interest. Scraped law snippet 1 and 2 do not contain any pertinent information. The change creates a contradiction because it deviates from the legal standard of requiring a material interest for a conflict of interest to exist.

perturbation.original_text: A breach of this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement may result in action being taken against the member (up to and including removal from the Board and/or SAMED Committee).
perturbation.changed_text: Any perceived deviation from this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement, as determined solely by the SAMED Executive Officer, will result in immediate and irreversible expulsion from the Board and/or SAMED Committee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for a range of actions and implies due process. The modified text dictates automatic expulsion based on the sole determination of the Executive Officer, removing due process and potentially conflicting with principles of fairness and natural justice under South African administrative law, which requires fair procedures in decision-making, particularly where rights are affected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Natural Justice/Administrative Justice Act 3 of 2000 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Administrative Justice Act and principles of natural justice require fair administrative procedures. The modified text removes any opportunity for explanation or appeal, contravening these principles. Natural justice dictates that people affected by the decision must be informed and able to respond to the decision being made.

( May 2021 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) The Promotion of Administrative Justice Act ( PAJA ) is an important piece of South African legislation , and the cornerstone of administrative law in South Africa . [ 1 ] Administrative action [ edit ] The South African Law Commission’s draft Administrative Justice Bill described the concept of "administrative action" widely: any action or decision performed by an organ of state or any exercise of public power other than executive, legislative or judicial action. The definition of administrative action ultimately enacted in PAJA was "considerably more complicated and qualified." [ 2 ] Section 1 of the PAJA defines administrative action as "any decision taken, or any failure to take a decision, by "an organ of state, when "exercising a power in terms of the Constitution or a provincial Constitution; or "exercising a public power or performing a public function in terms of any Legislation ; or "a natural or juristic person, other than an organ of state, when exercising a public power or performing a public function in terms of an empowering provision, which adversely affects the rights of any person and which ha
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts principles of natural justice and fair administrative procedure. The original text allows for action to be taken, implying due process, while the changed text dictates immediate expulsion based solely on the Executive Officer's determination. This removes the opportunity for explanation or appeal, directly contravening principles of fair procedure as described in the Law Explanation and as suggested by the South African legislation detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which addresses 'administrative action' and potentially affects the rights of a person.
